intro:
Jezreel (Hebrew Yizre'el, "God sows") names both a royal city of the northern kingdom and the great valley in which it lay — and, in the prophets, becomes a charged symbol of bloodshed, judgment, and the promise of God's renewed sowing. Few place-names in Scripture carry so heavy a freight of history and prophecy.

The city of Jezreel stood on the slopes between Mount Gilboa and the hill of Moreh, in the territory of Issachar (Joshua 19:18). It served as a second royal residence for the Omride dynasty; Ahab kept a palace there beside the vineyard of Naboth (1 Kings 21:1). It was here that Jezebel engineered Naboth's judicial murder so Ahab could seize his vineyard (1 Kings 21:15–16), drawing down Elijah's terrible word of doom on Ahab's house (1 Kings 21:23). That doom fell at Jezreel when Jehu rode in: Joram was struck down and thrown onto Naboth's plot, and Jezebel was flung from her window and trampled, her body devoured by dogs in fulfillment of prophecy (2 Kings 9:30–37). Jehu then massacred the rest of Ahab's seventy sons there (2 Kings 10:11).

The Valley of Jezreel (the Plain of Esdraelon) is one of the most strategic corridors of the ancient Near East, a broad fertile plain cutting across northern Israel between the coastal road and the Jordan, and so a perennial battlefield. There Deborah and Barak triumphed by the waters of Megiddo, and Gideon routed the Midianites who had encamped in the valley (Judges 6:33). On its eastern edge, by the spring of Jezreel, Gideon's army was sifted (Judges 7:1), and on Mount Gilboa above it Saul and Jonathan fell to the Philistines (1 Samuel 29:1). Its western gateway, Megiddo, gives its name to the climactic battle of Armageddon (Har-Megiddo) in Revelation 16:16.

Jezreel's deepest significance is prophetic. The prophet Hosea was told to name his firstborn son Jezreel, "for in just a little while I will punish the house of Jehu for the blood of Jezreel" (Hosea 1:4) — God's judgment on the dynasty's violence. Yet the same name, meaning "God sows," is turned to hope: the LORD promises a day of restoration when "I will sow her for myself in the land" and the scattered people will be regathered, "for great shall be the day of Jezreel" (Hosea 1:11; Hosea 2:22–23). Thus Jezreel holds together in one name the harvest of judgment for shed blood and the promise of God's new planting of a renewed people.
